Now let me look at the key UAF-prone areas. I've seen autoMemoryCollect which looks interesting. Let me check some specific areas:

1. `autoMemoryFreed` - There's a potential issue here: when `side=0`, it searches from `ctx->amqueue_used-1-j` down, and when `side=1`, it searches from `j` up. The count is `(ctx->amqueue_used+1)/2`. This is a "zig-zag" scan to find the most recently added items first. When it finds a match, it:
   - Marks it as `REDISMODULE_AM_FREED`
   - Swaps it with the last element in the queue
   - Decrements the queue counter

But there's a subtle bug lurking here - when marking an item as freed and then swapping it with the last element, if the item being freed happens to already be at the end of the queue, the swap is redundant but the decrement still happens. The real issue is that this doesn't properly handle the case where the freed item's position matters for subsequent operations.

Actually, looking more carefully at the logic: the code marks the type as freed first, then conditionally swaps only if the item isn't already at the end. If it is at the end, it just decrements without swapping. So the freed marker gets overwritten by the swap in most cases, which means the actual item at position `i` becomes whatever was at the end of the queue.

Now looking at `autoMemoryCollect`, it iterates through the queue and frees each item based on its type—clearing the auto-memory flag first, then going through and calling the appropriate cleanup function for strings, replies, keys, and so on.

What's interesting is that the flag gets re-enabled right before the queue itself is freed, which seems backwards at first but apparently allows items to call `autoMemoryFreed` during cleanup if needed. Though there might be an issue with this approach that I'm starting to see... I'm realizing the flag restoration happens right after the loop but before the queue is freed, which creates a window where new `autoMemoryAdd` calls could try to use an already-freed queue. The sequence seems contradictory—the flag is cleared to prevent the free operations from calling `autoMemoryFreed`, but then it's restored before the queue structure itself is deallocated, which defeats that purpose. Looking at the code flow more carefully: the flag is cleared before the loop to prevent freed items from trying to remove themselves from the queue, then restored afterward but before the queue itself is freed. The question is whether restoring the flag before `zfree(ctx->amqueue)` could cause issues if something tries to add to the queue during that final cleanup step. I'm checking whether the flag restoration at line 798 could trigger `autoMemoryAdd` calls before the queue is nullified—but `zfree` doesn't invoke module code, so that path is safe. 878→ *
   879→ * It is possible to call this function even when automatic memory management
   880→ * is enabled. In that case the string will be released ASAP and removed
   881→ * from the pool of string to release at the end.
   882→ *
   883→ * If the string was created with a NULL context 'ctx', it is also possible to
   884→ * pass ctx as NULL when releasing the string (but passing a context will not
   885→ * create any issue). Strings created with a context should be freed also passing
   886→ * the context, so if you want to free a string out of context later, make sure
   887→ * to create it using a NULL context. */
   888→void RM_FreeString(RedisModuleCtx *ctx, RedisModuleString *str) {
   889→    decrRefCount(str);
   890→    if (ctx != NULL) autoMemoryFreed(ctx,REDISMODULE_AM_STRING,str);
   891→}
   892→
   893→/* Every call to this function, will make the string 'str' requiring
   894→ * an additional call to RedisModule_FreeString() in order to really
   895→ * free the string. Note that the automatic freeing of the string obtained
   896→ * enabling modules automatic memory management counts for one
   897→ * RedisModule_FreeString() call (it is just executed automatically).
   898→ *
   899→ * Normally you want to call this function when, at the same time
   900→ * the following conditions are true:
   901→ *
   902→ * 1) You have automatic memory management enabled.
   903→ * 2) You want to create string objects.
   904→ * 3) Those string objects you create need to live *after* the callback
   905→ *    function(for example a command implementation) creating them returns.
   906→ *
   907→ * Usually you want this in order to store the created string object
   908→ * into your own data structure, for example when implementing a new data
   909→ * type.
   910→ *
   911→ * Note that when memory management is turned off, you don't need
   912→ * any call to RetainString() since creating a string will always result
   913→ * into a string that lives after the callback function returns, if
   914→ * no FreeString() call is performed.
   915→ *
   916→ * It is possible to call this function with a NULL context. */
   917→void RM_RetainString(RedisModuleCtx *ctx, RedisModuleString *str) {
   918→    if (ctx == NULL || !autoMemoryFreed(ctx,REDISMODULE_AM_STRING,str)) {
   919→        /* Increment the string reference counting only if we can't
   920→         * just remove the object from the list of objects that should
   921→         * be reclaimed. Why we do that, instead of just incrementing
   922→         * the refcount in any case, and let the automatic FreeString()
   923→         * call at the end to bring the refcount back at the desired
   924→         * value? Because this way we ensure that the object refcount
   925→         * value is 1 (instead of going to 2 to be dropped later to 1)
   926→         * after the call to this function. This is needed for functions
   927→         * like RedisModule_StringAppendBuffer() to work. */
   928→        incrRefCount(str);
   929→    }
   930→}
   931→
   932→/* Given a string module object, this function returns the string pointer
   933→ * and length of the string. The returned pointer and length should only
   934→ * be used for read only accesses and never modified. */
   935→const char *RM_StringPtrLen(const RedisModuleString *str, size_t *len) {
   936→    if (str == NULL) {
   937→        const char *errmsg = "(NULL string reply referenced in module)";
   938→        if (len) *len = strlen(errmsg);
   939→        return errmsg;
   940→    }
   941→    if (len) *len = sdslen(str->ptr);
   942→    return str->ptr;
   943→}

   951→ * as a valid, strict long long (no spaces before/after), REDISMODULE_ERR
